segments.s392-g9: چنین (thus) is an adverb placed before the verb to mark reported speech or manner. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here functioning as the main reporting verb); با + NP ('with/to the') introduces the addressee. نامور is an adjective 'renowned/famed' modifying the following epithet گرگسار, which is an attributive epithet 'wolf‑ish/ferocious' describing the addressee.
segments.s392-g10: که‌ای here is a poetic/colloquial combination (که + ای) that introduces the direct vocative address — effectively 'O ...' in older Persian speech. نامور repeats 'renowned' as a vocative adjective; شیردل is a compound adjective 'lion‑hearted' (brave) modifying شهریار, the noun 'king' (the person being addressed). The whole segment is the quoted vocative phrase addressed to the king.
segments.s392-g11: دگر here means 'another' or 'a different' and modifies منزلت 'rank/status/station'. شیری is an adjectival form 'leonine' (literally 'of a lion') modifying منزلت. آید is the present/historical‑present of آمدن 'to come' and takes the locative phrase به جنگ 'into/for battle' as its complement.
segments.s392-g12: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining the previous clause ('such that...'). با جنگ او = 'with/having his battle' (با marking accompaniment/means, and او = 'his'). برنتابد is a negative/disabling verb form meaning 'does not endure / cannot bear' (here third‑person singular present/historical sense). نهنگ is the patient/subject that 'cannot endure' — a large/overwhelming creature used figuratively.
segments.s392-g13: عقاب دلاور is a nominal phrase: عقاب 'eagle' headword modified by the adjective/epithet دلاور 'valiant'. بر آن راه = 'onto/against that path/way' with بر (on/upon) + demonstrative آن + راه 'path/way'. شیر here is genitival/appositive 'of the lion' (راهِ شیر = 'the lion's way').
segments.s392-g14: نپرد is a negative present/optative form (نـ + پَریدن stem) meaning 'will not fly/leap' or 'does not attack/approach' — the negation is fused to the verb. وگر is a conjunctive adversative 'but/and if' used here as 'even if' or 'however'. چند is functioning adverbially ('however/much/as much as') with باشد دلیر ('be he brave') forming a concessive clause 'even if he be brave'. باشد is the present/subjunctive of بودن 'to be', and دلیر is the predicate adjective 'brave'.
segments.s392-g15: بخندید is a past/historical present form of خندیدن 'to laugh' (here 'laughed'). روشن‌دل is a compound adjective 'clear‑hearted / bright‑minded' modifying the proper name اسفندیار (Esfandiyar), so the whole phrase names the laughing person.
segments.s392-g16: بدو is a prepositional/old pronominal phrase meaning 'to him' (به + او). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here used with the quoted vocative). کای is the vocative particle 'O' (classical/poetic). ترک is the noun 'Turk' (the person being addressed) and ناسازگار is an adjective meaning 'incompatible/hostile/uncooperative', used here as an insult or reproach.
segments.s392-g17: ببینی is the present/subjunctive-form (with the b- prefix) meaning 'you will see' or 'you see' addressed to تو; فردا 'tomorrow' is an adverb placed after the verb. که here is the subordinating conjunction 'that/where' introducing the subordinate clause that specifies what will be seen. با + نره‌شیر literally 'with the *nareh-shir*' — a compound epithet (here a man likened to a lion) introduced by the preposition با.
segments.s392-g18: چگونه 'how' introduces the manner clause. شوم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن 'to become' (here 'I shall become' / 'I become'); من 'I' is emphatic subject. به جنگش uses به + noun to mean 'in/into the fight with him' (جنگ + possessive -ش 'his' = 'the fight against him'). دلیر is a predicative adjective placed after the phrase and linked to شوم: 'I become brave.'
segments.s392-g19: چو here means 'when' (temporal). تاریک شد شب is 'night became dark' with the adjective تاریک and the past auxiliary شد; note the poetic order (adjective + شد + noun) where شب is the noun 'night'. بفرمود is an honorific/past form of گفتن/فرمودن meaning 'he ordered/said' and شاه (the subject) follows the verb — a common inversion in classical Persian poetry for emphasis.
segments.s392-g20: از آن جایگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from that place'; اندر آمد is a verbal compound meaning 'came forth / entered' with اندر 'in/within'; سپاه 'army' is the subject coming at the end (verb–subject order due to poetic inversion), so the whole line reads 'from that place the army came.'
segments.s392-g21: شب تیره 'dark night' is a noun followed by an attributive adjective. لشکر is the noun 'army/host.' همی is the old/progressive particle indicating continuous or ongoing action (here 'were doing' / 'kept on') and راندند is the past plural 'they drove/rode' (they advanced). The cluster therefore reads 'the host kept riding/advancing through the dark night.'
segments.s392-g22: بر او 'toward/on him' marks the target (هَم) of the following action; the second بر is a poetic repetition for emphasis/location. همی again marks continuous action. آفرین is the interjection/acclamation 'bravo' and خواندند is the past plural 'they called/shouted.' Word order places the prepositional phrase before the verb for poetic effect: 'upon him they repeatedly shouted “bravo.”'
segments.s392-g23: چو 'when/as' introduces a simile/temporal clause. خورشید 'the sun' is the agent; ز (poetic form of از) means 'from/out of' and links to آن چادر لاژورد 'that tent of lapis/azure' (لاژورد indicating the deep blue color). The phrase paints the image 'as the sun came out from that azure tent.'
segments.s392-g24: یکی 'one' or 'someone' is an indefinite subject. مطرفی کرد is a verbal phrase 'performed a diversion/made a display' (مطرفی here = a diverting/ornamental action); دیبای زرد is the direct object 'a yellow brocade/silk' with adjective after the noun (typical Persian order). The clause means 'one person made a display with a yellow brocade.'
segments.s392-g49: This clause has the preposition-like particle بر before the name to mark the target of the acclamation, then the noun (name) and the past plural verb خواندند 'they uttered/said'. آفرین is used as an interjection/nominal acclamation ('bravo'). The verb خواندند is past, third-person plural, describing a completed action (they cried/sang/uttered the acclamation).
segments.s392-g50: ورا is the unstressed object pronoun 'to him' immediately following the verb cluster; نامدار زمین is a compound epithet meaning 'renowned of the earth' (an epithet in postpositive order), and خواندند here means 'they called (him) [by that name]'. The verb is again past plural and takes ورا as its object.
segments.s392-g51: وز is a poetic/conjunctive particle here introducing the move from the previous place (classical 'وز' ~ 'از' / 'and from'), followed by آنجا 'there' and the past verb بیامد 'came'. The sequence کی رهنمای is a proper-name/title (Ki/Kay Rahnamāy) functioning as the subject; the verb is perfective past 'he/they came'.
segments.s392-g52: به نزدیک is a locative phrase meaning 'to/toward the vicinity of' (به + نزدیک), followed by two coordinated place-nouns: خرگاه and پرده‌سرای, joined by و. The preposition به + noun indicates direction/approach and نزدیک qualifies that place as 'near/close'.
segments.s392-g53: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed/laid (out)' (here used to mean 'set' as in setting a table), followed by خوان 'the table/feast' and a coordinated noun phrase خورشهای نغز 'delightful dishes.' The word order is verb — direct object(s), typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s392-g54: بیاورد is a past-subjunctive/prehistoric-narrative form here rendered 'he/they brought' (past action). It is followed by the agent/title سالار 'the commander/chief' and the adjective-noun phrase پاکیزه مغز 'pure/clean-minded' (an epithet). The verb takes this phrase as what was brought or presented; the compact word order (verb then subject/title then adjective–noun) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s392-g25: سپهبد is the subject (a title: spahbed). The prepositional phrase به جای دلیران ('to the place/position of the brave') uses به + noun phrase, with جای as noun 'place' followed by the genitive plural دلیران. The verb رسید (past 3rd sg.) comes at the end, giving the simple past 'reached'.
segments.s392-g26: This coordinate phrase also takes رسید as its verb (repeated at the end): به هامون و پرخاش شیران reached. هامون is a place-name/common noun 'plain'; و coordinates two objects ('and'); پرخاش شیران is a noun phrase 'the attack/aggression of the lions' (پرخاش = assault/aggression, شیران = lions), all governed by the final رسید.
segments.s392-g27: پشوتن is the proper-name subject. بفرمود is the honorific/polite past verb 'ordered/commanded'. تا introduces the subordinate clause of result/purpose ('that/so that'), followed by رفت (past 'went/go') and the adverb پیش ('forward' / 'before'), so the clause means 'he ordered (that) he go forward' or 'ordered him to go ahead.'
segments.s392-g28: ورا is the literary object clitic 'to him'. پندها is the plural noun 'admonitions / pieces of advice'. داد is the past verb 'gave'. ز (poetic) governs the following noun phrase and here introduces the matter/ground ('concerning / from'), اندازه بیش together means 'beyond measure' or 'excess'—اندازه here 'measure/moderation' and بیش 'excess/too much'. Thus: 'he gave him admonitions about/exhortations against exceeding measure.'
segments.s392-g29: بدو گفت is the light-verb phrase 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). The clitic/particle ک(این) (here written کاین) introduces the quoted/content clause 'that this army...'. لشکر is the noun 'army' and سرفراز is a postpositive adjective 'proud/victorious' modifying it.
segments.s392-g30: سپردم is the 1st-person past 'I entrusted/handed over.' The sequence ت + را represents the enclitic object 'ترا' (you as direct object, with را as object marker). من is the emphatic subject 'I'. شدم + رزمساز is 'I became (a) rasm-saz' — literally 'battle‑maker' / one who makes or instigates war; the verb شدم is past 'became.'
segments.s392-g31: بیامد is the past 'he came'. چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when / as'. با شیر here indicates proximity/encounter 'to/with the lion' and modifies the verb نزدیک شد ('became near' = 'approached'); note the verb نزدیک شد is a light copular construction 'became near/approached' with the verb at the end.
segments.s392-g32: جهان is the subject 'the world / existence'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on'. دل شیر is a genitival phrase 'the lion's heart' (دل = heart; شیر = lion). تاریک شد is the predicate 'became dark' (تاریک adjective + شد 'became'), so literally 'the world became dark upon the lion's heart.'
segments.s392-g41: This is an SOV-style clause with the preposition + goal first: به ریگ اندر افگند = 'he cast into the sand'. اندر is the archaic poetic preposition 'in/into' and افگند is the past tense verb 'threw/cast'. غلتان is a participial/adverbial modifier 'rolling' that follows the verb phrase but here interposes before the object complement سرش ('his head'), where ش is the 3rd-person enclitic pronoun. Literal order: 'to sand into cast rolling head-his.'
segments.s392-g42: ز خون لعل شد دست = 'from the blood the hand became ruby-like': ز introduces the source ('from'); لعل شد is a predicate meaning 'became (like) a ruby' with شد as the past of شدن. The second chunk و جنگی برش is elliptical/compact: و = 'and', جنگی is an adjective or substantive 'warlike/fighter', while برش appears here as a fused form (بر + ش) or an unclear nominal; the clause ends with a verbless or truncated tag — the poetic compression leaves the exact relation of جنگی/برش dependent on context (instrument/locative or an object), so the line conveys bloodied hand and a warlike detail appended.
segments.s392-g43: به آب اندر آمد = 'he came into the water': the prepositional phrase appears before آمد (past of آمدن). سر و تن بشست places the direct objects (سر 'head' and تن 'body') before the transitive past بشست 'he washed', following classical Persian object-before-verb ordering in poetry.
segments.s392-g44: نگهدار جز پاک یزدان نجست = 'he sought no guardian except the Pure God': نگهدار is the noun 'guardian/protector' and نجست (from جستن) is past 'sought'. جز introduces the exception 'except', so the sense is negative/limiting: he sought no helper other than God. Note the verb نجست governs the whole phrase; the construction implies absence of any guardian aside from (پاک) یزدان.
segments.s392-g45: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (formulaic opening). کای is the vocative 'O', and داور here addresses the divine/judicial figure; داد و پاک are nouns/adjectives in apposition ('justice and pure'), forming a laudatory vocative phrase 'O Judge, (of) justice and purity.' Note that poetic vocatives stack nouns/adjectives without linking verbs.
segments.s392-g46: به دستم ددان را تو کردی هلاک = 'you made the d... perish by my hand': به دستم is an instrumental/agent phrase 'by/with my hand' (به used instrumentally), ددان را is the definite object marked by را (here the target word ددان is an archaic or poetic noun not matched by the provided senses), تو کردی is the 2nd-person past 'you did/made', and هلاک is the result 'ruin/destruction' — together 'you caused X to be destroyed by my hand.'
segments.s392-g47: هم اندر زمان لشکر آنجا رسید = 'also/at the same time the army arrived there': هم اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase meaning 'at the same time', لشکر is the subject 'the army' and رسید is the past 'arrived' (typical verb-final feel in epic Persian lines).
segments.s392-g48: پشوتن سر و یال شیران بدید = 'Peshotan saw the heads and manes of the lions': پشوتن is the proper-name subject, سر و یال شیران ('the heads and manes of the lions') is the compound object (شیران follows to mark possession), and بدید is the past 'saw'. The noun cluster places coordinated objects before the verb, as is common in the verse.
segments.s392-g33: This is an inverted, somewhat archaic copular clause: 'یکی بود' = 'there was one', with 'بود' (past of 'to be') following 'یکی'. 'نر' and 'ماده' specify the sexes and both modify the final noun 'شیر' (lion), so the literal order is 'one was male and the other (was) female — (a) lion'.
segments.s392-g34: 'برفتند' is past plural (بر‑ + رفتند) meaning 'they went forth/advanced' and serves as the main verb for the plural subject just introduced. The adjectives 'پرخاشجوی و دلیر' follow the verb as predicative descriptions ('aggressive and brave').
segments.s392-g35: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'; 'نر اندر آمد' = 'the male came in/approached' (note archaic 'اندر' = 'into/within'). 'یکی تیغ زد' places the indefinite 'یکی' ('one/someone') before the past verb 'زد' = 'struck (with a blade)'.
segments.s392-g36: 'ببد' is an archaic/periphrastic past form meaning 'became/was'; the phrase 'ریگ زیرش' = 'the sand beneath him' (زیر + possessive -ش). 'به سان' = 'as/like' and 'بسد' (infinitive/old conj.) means 'to suffice' or 'is enough' — together expressing that the sand under him became (or served) as something sufficient/adequate (poetic compression of result).
segments.s392-g37: 'ز' (poetic 'from') + 'سر' ('head') and 'تا' ('to/until') + 'میانش' ('his middle', میان + possessive -ش) mark the extent. 'به دو نیم گشت' = 'was/turned into two halves' where 'گشت' is past 'became/was turned' indicating the resulting state (i.e., he was split).
segments.s392-g38: Noun phrase 'دل شیر ماده' = 'the heart of the female lion' (head noun first, followed by its qualifier/possessor). 'پر از بیم' = 'full of fear' ('پر' = full, governed by preposition 'از'), and 'گشت' again is past 'became' giving the resulting emotional state.
segments.s392-g39: 'چو' = 'when'; 'جفتش' is 'his/her mate' (جفت + possessive -ش). 'برآشفت' is a prefixed past verb meaning 'became enraged/rose in anger', and 'و آمد فراز' = 'and came up/forward' where 'فراز' is an adverbial 'up/on high' (poetic placement after the verb).
segments.s392-g40: Here 'یکی' = 'someone/one' is the subject; 'تیغ زد' = 'struck with a blade' (past). 'بر سرش' = 'on/against his head' with the possessive '-ش', and the final noun 'رزمساز' is an appositive identifying that 'one' as 'the warrior' (postposed apposition common in poetry).
segments.s392-g1: غم آمد (= 'grief came') is a common Persian idiom meaning 'sorrow befell' or 'grief arrived'; آمد is simple past 'came'. همه بهره is a noun phrase 'the whole share/portion' with همه (whole/entire) preceding the head بهره (share). گرگسار here names the person/place whose portion is affected; its position after the noun marks possession/association.
segments.s392-g2: ز marks source or cause here ('because of' / 'from'), introducing the agents of the grief. گرگان جنگی is an adjectival phrase: گرگان as the place-name 'Gorgan' (or its people) followed by جنگی 'warlike/warriors'. و links اسفندیار as an additional cause.
segments.s392-g3: یکی خوان زرین biarastand is a compact clause: یکی 'one/a single' quantifies خوان 'banquet/table', زرین is the adjective 'golden' placed after the noun (typical Persian order). بیاراستند is 3rd-person plural past 'they adorned/set out' (an older/poetic form of آراستن).
segments.s392-g4: خورشها بخوردند is parsed as 'the dishes were eaten/they ate the dishes' with خورشها (plural) as object/topic and بخوردند the 3rd-plural past of خوردن 'to eat' (here meaning 'they ate' / 'they partook'). و connects to می‌خواستند, an imperfect/continuous past 'they were wanting/intending' — here 'they were about to (do something)' linking to the next action.
segments.s392-g5: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said (to do)'. تا introduces the subordinate clause of command ('that/so that') and بسته را is the definite object 'the package' marked by را. پیش اوی is a prepositional phrase 'in his presence / before him' (اوی = archaic 'him').
segments.s392-g6: ببردند is the past 3rd-person-plural ('they carried/took') used to report the commanded action. لرزان and پرآب روی are adjectival phrases describing the carriers: لرزان 'trembling' and پرآب روی literally 'with faces full of water' (= tearful/with watery eyes). The adjectives follow the verb as a descriptive circumstantial clause.
segments.s392-g7: سه جام میش داد is a compact action: سه جام 'three cups' as the quantity + container noun, followed by میش (textual/lexical difficulty — here a content word whose conventional meanings listed do not clearly match the context) and داد 'he gave' (past). و پرسش گرفت is idiomatic: 'and he took up/posed a question' (پرسش 'question', گرفت 'took' used in the sense 'began/raised').
segments.s392-g8: که introduces reported speech 'that/that is (he said)'; اکنون 'now'; چه گویی literally 'what do you say?' — گویی here is the verb 'you say' (the lemma/senses provided for this token in the list don't capture the verbal form, so sense is marked -1). The second چه is exclamatory 'what (a...)' and بینم شگفت is 'I see (this) wondrous/marvelous' (بینم is present-subjunctive/first-person of دیدن used in exclamatory/reporting clauses; شگفت is 'wonder/wondrous').

sentences.s392-s5: Thus he said to the famed, wolfish one: "O famed, lion‑hearted king!"
sentences.s392-s6: Another leonine rank will come into battle, one whose fighting the whale cannot endure.
sentences.s392-s7: The valiant eagle will not fly onto that lion's path, however brave he may be.
sentences.s392-s8: The clear‑hearted Esfandiyar laughed, and to him said, 'O uncooperative Turk!'
sentences.s392-s9: You will see tomorrow how I shall become brave against that lion in fighting him.
sentences.s392-s10: When night grew dark the king commanded,
sentences.s392-s11: In the dark night the host rode on, and upon him they cried 'bravo' repeatedly.
sentences.s392-s12: When the sun from that lapis-blue tent (appeared), one of them made a show with a yellow brocade.
sentences.s392-s25: They cried "Bravo!" for Esfandiyar, and they called him the famed (renowned) of the earth.
sentences.s392-s26: From there came Ki Rahnamay toward the vicinity of the khargah and the curtained pavilion.
sentences.s392-s27: They set the table and (laid out) delightful dishes; the commander brought forth a pure-minded one.
sentences.s392-s13: The spahbed reached the place of the brave and came upon the plain and the lions' assault.
sentences.s392-s14: Peshotan ordered him to go forward, and gave him admonitions about exceeding the proper measure.
sentences.s392-s15: He said to him, 'I have entrusted this proud army to you; I have become a maker of battle.'
sentences.s392-s16: He came; when he drew near the lion, the world grew dark on the lion's heart.
sentences.s392-s21: He cast his head, rolling, into the sand; from the blood his hand became like a ruby, and (—) on him.
sentences.s392-s22: He went into the water and washed his head and body; he sought no guardian except the Pure God.
sentences.s392-s23: Thus he said: 'O Judge, (source of) justice and purity, you by my hand caused the demons to perish.'
sentences.s392-s24: At the same time the army reached there; Peshotan saw the heads and manes of the lions.
sentences.s392-s17: There was a male and another a female lion; they went forward, quarrelsome and brave.
sentences.s392-s18: When the male came forward, one struck with a blade; the sand beneath him became (so), sufficient as it were.
sentences.s392-s19: From his head to his middle he was split in two; the heart of the female lion became full of fear.
sentences.s392-s20: When her mate grew angry and came up, one — a warrior — struck at his head with a blade.
sentences.s392-s1: Grief came upon the whole share of Gorgasar, from the warriors of Gorgan and Esfandiyar.
sentences.s392-s2: They set a single golden banquet; the dishes were served and they were about to eat.
sentences.s392-s3: He ordered that they take the sealed package before him, trembling and with tear-filled faces.
sentences.s392-s4: He gave three cups (of drink) and raised a question: 'Now what do you say? What wondrous thing do I see?'
